Prompt Engineering  Introduction to Generative AI Business Applications

What is Generative AI? • AI technology that produces new and original content (e.g. text, images, audio and synthetic data) • Designed to learn underlying patterns and structures in data to generate novel outputs that mimic human creativity • ChatGPT: A well-known example of GenAI, but the field extends far beyond text generation to include various applications • Text Generation: Write articles, stories, and scripts • Image Synthesis: Create realistic images from scratch • Music Composition: Generate original music tracks in various genres and styles

Main Types of Generative AI • Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs) • Variational Autoencoders (VAEs) • Diffusion Models • Transformer-based Models

Generative Adversarial Networks (GAN) • Two neural networks that compete with each other:

➢ The Generator Creates new data instances (fake) Generator tries to trick Discriminator

➢ The Discriminator Evaluates the generated data to identify the fake Discriminator tries to outsmart Generator

Source: https://semiengineering.com/knowledge_centers/artificial-intelligence/neuralnetworks/generative-adversarial-network-gan/ 6  Applications of Generative Adversarial Networks

• Photo Editing: edit photographs in various ways, such as changing the background, adding or removing objects, or altering the appearance of people or animals in the image

Source: https://gandissect.csail.mit.edu Source: https://youtu.be/oGGTC0Gmplw 7  Applications of Generative Adversarial Networks • Generate images: generate realistic photographs of various objects and scenes, including landscapes, animals, and architecture. Image to Image Text to Image

Source: Nvidia StyleGAN2 https://youtu.be/9QuDh3W3lOY Source: Nvidia StyleGAN-T https://youtu.be/0NHnmVTTsjU 8  Variational Autoencoders (VAEs) • Two neural networks that complement each other:

➢ The Encoder (or Recognition Model) Encode input data into a set of latent attributes

➢ The Decoder (or Generative Model) Decode latent attributes to generate new data

• Generates new images and time series data

• Specific types of autoencoders for generative AI • Undercomplete Autoencoders for dimensionality reduction (compression) • Denoising Autoencoders for removing noise from images or audio  Applications of Denoising Autoencoders • Noise Cancelling Earbuds using AI to recognize your voice while eliminating noises and other voices around you

Source: https://youtu.be/lxOKunnPLgg 10  Diffusion Models • Gradually adds noise to data until it becomes pure noise, and then learns to reverse this process (reverse/denoising process).

Image Source: CMSWire 11  Application of Diffusion Models Google TryOnDiffusion allows to virtually try on clothes

Source: https://youtu.be/cYu2k800_jg 12  Application of Diffusion Models

Source: https://blog.google/products/shopping/virtual-try-on-google-generative-ai/ 13  Transformer-based Models • Relies on self-attention mechanisms to process input data • Key Concepts • Self-attention: allows the model to weigh the importance of different words in a sentence relative to each other (attention score) • Multi-Head Attention: capture different types of relationships between words • Positional Encoding: provide information about the position of each word in the sequence • Particularly effective for sequence data (e.g. text) https://arxiv.org/abs/1706.03762  Transformer-based Models • Two neural networks that complement each other:

➢ The Encoder The encoder processes the input sequence into continuous representations using self-attention and feed-forward networks, providing a rich context for each word

➢ The Decoder The decoder generates the output sequence by attending to the encoder's representations and the previously generated tokens, ensuring coherence and relevance to the input • Main application: Text generation • E.g. ChatGPT, Language translation, Code generation

Application of Transformer Models

Large Language Models

Large Language Models (LLMs) AI systems based on ML algorithms trained on vast text data • Generate human-like language • Leverage architectures to perform NLP tasks Examples • GPT (OpenAI): Text generation, translation, summarisation, image analysis • BERT (Google): Context understanding, question answering, sentiment analysis • Gemini (Google): Text generation, translation, summarisation, image analysis • RoBERTa (Facebook AI): Optimised BERT, improved NLP performance

Pre-trained models GPT-3, GPT-3.5, GPT-4, T5 and Gemini trained on vast amounts of data • They serve as a foundation for further training and fine-tuning for specific tasks • Adapt to various applications such as text generation and translation

Capabilities • Learn a wide range of language patterns and structures • Generate coherent and grammatically correct text on a various topics • Understand context and nuances in language

A brief history of GPT GPT-4 GPT-3 GPT-3.5 ChatGPT Multimodal 175 billion 175 billion 175 billion

WebGPT 175 billion

GPT-2 InstructGPT 1.2 billion 1.3 billion GPT 117 million

BERTBase Transformers 110million Vision Transformers

2017 2018 2019 2020 2021 2022 2023  Attention A High-Level Look

Transformer models are a type of neural network designed to process sequences — transforming an input sequence to an output sequence. The encoder-decoder architecture  Transformers — Learning Context Self-Attention Self-Attention

“The animal didn't cross the street because it was too tired ”

What does “it” in this sentence refer to? Multi-Head Attention • It expands the model’s ability to focus on different positions. • It gives the attention layer multiple “representation subspaces”.   GPT-1 Unsupervised pre-training • Given an unsupervised corpus of tokens U = {u 1, , un}, we use a standard language modeling objective to maximize the following likelihood:

• Where k is the size of the context window.

• It is an autoregressive language model. • Given an initial text as prompt, it will produce text that continues the prompt.

Radford, Alec; Narasimhan, Karthik; Salimans, Tim; Sutskever, Ilya (June 11, 2018). "Improving Language Understanding by Generative Pre-Training"   GPT-1 Supervised fine-tuning • We assume a labeled dataset C, where each instance consists of a sequence of input tokens, x1, , xm, along with a label y.

• The objective is given as follows:

• Overall objective

• (a) improving generalization of the supervised model, and (b) accelerating convergence.

Radford, Alec; Narasimhan, Karthik; Salimans, Tim; Sutskever, Ilya (June 11, 2018). "Improving Language Understanding by Generative Pre-Training"   GPT-2 A few modifications to GPT-1 • Layer normalization was moved to the input of each sub-block. • An additional layer normalization added after the final self- attention block. • A modified initialization which accounts for the accumulation on the residual path with model depth is used. • We scale the weights of residual layers at initialization by a factor of 1/ N where N is the number of residual layers. • The vocabulary is expanded to 50,257. We also increase the context size from 512 to 1024 tokens. • A larger batch size of 512 is used.

Radford, Alec; Wu, Jeffrey; Child, Rewon; Luan, David; Amodei, Dario; Sutskever, Ilua (14 February 2019). "Language models are unsupervised multitask learners"  GPT-2 Training Dataset – Webtext

• Over 8 million documents

• A total of 40 GB of text

• All Wikipedia documents removed from WebText  GPT-2 Variants

GPT BERTLARGE GPT-2 The same model and architecture as GPT-2 • Modified initialization. • Reversible tokenization. • Alternating dense and locally banded sparse attention patterns. • 175 billion parameters. • It was estimated to cost 355 GPU years and cost $4.6m.

GPT-3 is applied without any gradient updates or fine-tuning • Tasks and few-shot demonstrations specified purely via text interaction with the model. Overview

Unsupervised Pre-training

The dataset of 300 billion tokens of text is used to generate training examples. GPT3 generates one token at a time (let’s assume a token is a word for now).  From GPT-1 to GPT-3

architecture parameter count training data GPT-1 12-level, 12-headed 0.12 billion BookCorpus: 4.5 GB of Transformer decoder text, from 7000 (no encoder), followed unpublished books of by linear-softmax. various genres. GPT-2 GPT-1, but with 1.5 billion WebText: 40 GB of text, modified normalization 8 million documents, from 45 million webpages upvoted on Reddit. GPT-3 GPT-2, but with 175 billion 570 GB plaintext, 0.4 modification to allow trillion tokens. Mostly larger scaling. CommonCrawl, WebText, English Wikipedia, and two books corpora (Books1 and Books2).  GPT-3 abilities

Larger models make increasingly efficient use of in-context information Limitations

• These models can also generate outputs that are untruthful, toxic, or reflect harmful sentiments.

• This is in part because GPT-3 is trained to predict the next word on a large dataset of Internet text, rather than to safely perform the language task that the user wants.  GPT-3.5 GPT-3.5 models use the same pre-training datasets as GPT-3, with additional fine-tuning.

This fine-tuning stage adds a concept called ‘reinforcement learning with human feedback’ or RLHF to the GPT-3 model.

The following models are in the GPT-3.5 series: • code-davinci-002 is a base model, so good for pure code-completion tasks • text-davinci-002 is an InstructGPT model based on code-davinci-002 • text-davinci-003 is an improvement on text-davinci-002  InstructGPT Aligning Language Models to Follow Instructions

Helpful - they should help the user solve their task

Honest - they shouldn’t fabricate information or mislead the user

Harmless - they should not cause physical, psychological, or social harm to people or the environment  InstructGPT  InstructGPT Step 1: Collect demonstration data, and train a supervised policy. • Our labelers provide demonstrations of the desired behavior on the input prompt distribution. We then fine-tune a pretrained GPT-3 model on this data using supervised learning. Step 2: Collect comparison data, and train a reward model. • We collect a dataset of comparisons between model outputs, where labelers indicate which output they prefer for a given input. We then train a reward model to predict the human-preferred output. Step 3: Optimize a policy against the reward model using PPO. • We use the output of the RM as a scalar reward. We fine-tune the supervised policy to optimize this reward using the PPO algorithm   GPT – Generative Pre-trained Transformers Model architecture • Transformers Unsupervised pre-training • on a diverse corpus of unlabeled text • to learn the initial parameters of a neural network model Supervised fine-tuning (SFT) • on each specific task • adapt these parameters to a target task using the corresponding supervised objective.

Radford, Alec; Narasimhan, Karthik; Salimans, Tim; Sutskever, Ilya (June 11, 2018). "Improving Language Understanding by Generative Pre-Training" Fine-tuned models BERT, RoBERTa, and ALBERT • Pre-trained on large datasets (e.g., Wikipedia, BooksCorpus) • Fine-tuned on smaller, task-specific datasets

Capabilities • Highly effective for tasks like sentiment analysis, question-answering, text classification • Used in industrial applications requiring task-specific language models • Examples: Customer support bots, content moderation, medical diagnosis assistance

Multimodal models CLIP (Contrastive Language-Image Pre-Training), DALL-E • Combine text with other modalities like images or videos • Create more robust and versatile language models

Capabilities • Understand relationships between images and text • Generate text descriptions from images • Generate images from textual descriptions • Creative content generation (e.g. artwork, visual stories), enhanced image and captioning, image classification

Generates the same output for the same given input Generates a different output every time for the same given input 64  Prompt Engineering • What is prompt engineering? • The process of refining a generative AI prompt to improve its accuracy and effectiveness • Why is it important in generative AI? • Enhances the quality of AI-generated responses • Improves user satisfaction and experience • Increases the reliability and consistency of AI outputs Prompt engineer jobs • High demand for experts in creating and optimising AI prompts • Key role in developing advanced AI applications

Source: https://time.com/6272103/ai-prompt-engineer-job/

Prompt Engineering • How to engineer generative AI prompts • Express Clearly: Formulate your query as clearly as possible • Experiment: Test different prompts to find the best practices • Follow up: Provide additional instructions or questions to refine responses • Play with Techniques: Try various prompting techniques to achieve the desired outcomes

• Zero-shot prompting • Provide direct instructions or questions to the AI without any prior context or examples • Best for relatively simple and straightforward tasks

• Examples • Prompt: Tell a joke • Prompt: Summarise the following paragraph: … • Prompt: Translate the following text to French: … • Prompt: Classify this document into research paper, white paper, or blog post

• Few-shot prompting • Include examples to help guide the LLM output • More suitable for complex tasks

• Examples • Tell a joke. For ex.: Knock, knock! Who's there? [...] • Here are some quotes from Shakespeare's plays. 1. "To be, or not to be, that is the question:" 2. "All the world's a stage, and all the men and women merely players." 3. "We are such stuff as dreams are made on, and our little life is rounded with a sleep." Generate text on making breakfast in the style of Shakespeare.

• Chain-of-thought (CoT) prompting • Break down complex reasoning into intermediate steps • Produce more accurate results by processing it step by step • Example You are a virtual assistant helping a user plan their day. Start by asking the user about their priorities for the day. Once you have this information, guide them create a schedule, that includes time for work, breaks, and any specific tasks they mentioned. Additionally, inquire about potential challenges or changes that might happen during the day and how the user plans to adapt their schedule accordingly.

• Prompt chaining • Split a complex task into smaller (and easier) subtasks • The LLM would respond to each subtask separately • Use the outputs to accomplish the overarching task • Best for the most complicated tasks • Example Prompt 1: Prompt 2: You are a librarian, and an expert on recommending Here's a list of recommended books: {list_of_book} books. Your role is to find the best book Arrange the list in alphabetical order by book name. recommendation based on the user's interests. This is user input: {user_input} Based on the input, retrieve books from the database Prompt 3: that aligns with the user's interest. Output is a list of To each book in {arranched_list_of_book}, add from 1 to 5 books (name, author, year of publication). stars indicating how good said book is (5 stars being the best).

Case Study

Using Generative AI in the Food Industry at

Use Case: Hello Fresh https://www.hellofresh.com.au/

Apply Generative AI in the Food Industry In a hypothetical scenario, you are working as a business analyst for Hello Fresh. Your manager has requested recommendations on enhancing Hello Fresh’s recipe offerings to boost online sales. To achieve this, you decide to leverage Generative AI tools, such as Gemini, to analyse existing recipes for their nutritional content and suggest healthier options. This includes recommending new healthy recipes, reducing salt and sugar content, substituting high-calorie ingredients with healthier alternatives, and ensuring balanced macronutrients.

Online healthy recipes + delivery of ingredients

Apply Generative AI in the Food Industry • In what ways can Generative AI enhance Hello Fresh's recipe offerings? • What types of Generative AI are suitable for this purpose? • What new features can Generative AI introduce to the Hello Fresh website?
